focus: A2 timeout hardening; A3 concurrency started
noveltyJustification: Converted A1's async-job direction into a concrete phased queue/checkpoint/cursor/retry contract and identified a retention edge case for large provider-outage backlogs.

summary: Current memory_index_scan is bounded only by batch concurrency, not by total request work; discovery, all file batches, post-processing, and lease completion happen before response.
Deferred indexing already commits memory_index/content_text/projection/BM25-visible rows with embedding_status pending; scan should use this boundary by default for large or async jobs.
Scan jobs should mirror embedder_jobs progress shape but use durable file work items for lexical work and memory row ids for vector work; terminal work items are never redone on resume.
Use bounded vector drain ticks after lexical commit: default row batch 50, while provider calls remain chunked by SPECKIT_EMBED_CLIENT_MAX_BATCH 256 and byte limits.

A large clean pending backlog is safe from retention only while it stays pending; moving many rows to retry during circuit-open periods can subject them to max-age or pending-cap failure, so drain must check provider availability before claiming clean pending rows.
Continuation cursors should reference persisted job state and phase, not raw file lists, so bounded synchronous compatibility can resume after process restart without duplicating committed work.
Concurrent same-scope callers should receive the same scan job with coalesced=true; the DB lease/cooldown remains an internal worker-start guard rather than caller-visible E429.
Keeping force:true as one request-bound scan is ruled out because it preserves the timeout class even if batch size is tuned; bounded jobs are required for corpus-size independence.
